title: Spiced red cabbage
subtitle: Braised red cabbage with apple, cider vinegar, and warm spices.
excerpt: Red cabbage slow-braised with apple, cider vinegar, brown sugar, cinnamon, and star anise. Cooked for an hour until tender, sweet, and sharp. The essential Christmas side dish that keeps for a week and reheats better than most things.
sourceNotes: Braised red cabbage with apple is a fixture of British Christmas cooking with roots in both the German Rotkohl and the Nordic rödbete traditions of slow-cooked spiced cabbage. The dish entered British cookery through the nineteenth century when German Christmas customs became popular, partly through the influence of Prince Albert. The version that is now standard in British kitchens uses cider vinegar and brown sugar in a combination that is as much British as German.

content.text: Spiced red cabbage is one of the few Christmas side dishes that improves over a few days. The vinegar, sugar, and spice continue to meld after the first day, and what starts out sharp and sweet becomes deeper and more rounded on the second reheating. Make it two days before Christmas.
The cabbage needs to be shredded fairly finely: thick chunks stay hard. A sharp knife and a steady hand take 10 minutes. A food processor with a slicing blade takes two.
A large heavy-based saucepan with a lid.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened.

Add the shredded cabbage, apple, {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of brown sugar, cinnamon stick, star anise, and cloves. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Stir well, then cover with a lid and cook over a medium-low heat for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring every 15 minutes, until the cabbage is completely tender and the liquid has reduced to a glaze.
Remove the cinnamon stick, star anise, and cloves. Taste and adjust the balance of vinegar and sugar. Serve hot, or cool and refrigerate for up to a week.

items.fix: Continue cooking with the lid on, adding a splash of water if it looks dry. Red cabbage takes longer to soften than green.
items.cause: Shredded too thickly, or the lid was not on properly.
items.symptom: Cabbage is still firm after an hour

items.fix: A little mushiness is acceptable in this dish; if very soft, serve straight away rather than reheating further.
Add a little more brown sugar and cook for another 5 minutes to integrate.
items.cause: Too much vinegar, or the apples were very tart.

items.fix: Remove the whole spices earlier next time. Cloves are particularly potent and 4 is already at the upper limit.
items.cause: Too many cloves or left the whole spices in too long.

content.text: Spiced red cabbage occupies a specific place in British Christmas cooking: it is the side dish that holds the table together when everything else is frantic. It takes no attention once in the pan, reheats without deteriorating, and pairs with almost every roast meat on the table. Its German and Nordic origins sit comfortably in a British Christmas that has absorbed German Christmas traditions since the Victorian era. The smell of it cooking on the hob is one of the reliable scent-markers of the season.
